subtopics.description: Understand what the participant does at work and how long they have been in this profession.
coverage_criteria: The participant has named their job title or role (e.g. 'nurse', 'software engineer', 'PhD student'). Any brief mention is sufficient — do not probe for more detail.
It is clear what field or industry the participant works in.

The participant has indicated approximately how long they have been in this specific role or job — a rough range is sufficient (e.g. 'a few years', '10 years', 'just started'). Any single duration answer satisfies this criterion. If asking, ask about THIS ROLE specifically (e.g. 'how long have you been in this role?') — do NOT ask about the broader field, discipline, or career category. Once answered, do NOT follow up on duration in any way: do NOT ask about career history, what they did before this role, whether their focus has changed, or anything about experience outside this role.

The participant has named at least one activity or task they do at work. Any mention of an activity counts — e.g. 'write code', 'attend meetings', 'manage projects', 'teach classes'. Do NOT ask for more specifics, more examples, or a deeper description of what the tasks involve.

subtopics.description: Post-widget task completeness sweep: after the task widget, confirm no tasks were missed and probe any new task the user mentions for clear action and object.
coverage_criteria: The user has been given the opportunity to name any tasks not captured by the widget. If they name a new task, its action and object are clear — probe once if either is vague, then accept and move on.

The user has signaled that the task list is complete. Detect any closure signal — whether negative ('no', 'nothing else') or affirmative ('that covers it', 'pretty much all we do', 'you have everything'). Do NOT pattern-match on specific words; infer the intent. One closure signal is final.
